vol_description:
2024 Sept 24, v0.1.1
0.1.1 mod 2024 Oct 25 to add information about textgrid file

D. Swingley 

The files here are the release version of the Philadelphia Home Corpus
recordings and annotations, up to the date of submission.  We are
transcribing corpus sessions continuously and confirming first release
versions for upload on a rolling basis.  The uploads do lag behind our
progress substantially as of Sept 2024.

The Philadelphia Home Corpus is being collected at the Department of
Psychology at the University of Pennsylvania, by Daniel Swingley. 
Funding was provided by the University and by NSF grant
SBE 1917608. Parents and children are African-American and living in
or near Philadelphia, Pennsylvania. The planned schedule of sessions
was:

- 6 months
  o session 1: audio recording with headset mic 1 hour
  o session 2: audio recording with headset mic 1 hour
  o Lena: audio recording with Lena device, full day

- 10 months
  o session 1: audio recording with headset mic 1 hour
  o session 2: audio recording with headset mic & video with camera on
               tripod 1 hour
  o Lena: audio recording with Lena device, full day

- 14 months
  o session 1: audio recording with headset mic 1 hour
  o session 2: audio recording with headset mic & video with camera on
               tripod 1 hour
  o Lena: audio recording with Lena device, full day

- 18 months: CDI administration
- 24 months: CDI administration

Our original plans also called for an in-lab visit to Penn for a
language-guided looking word-recognition experimental
session. Ultimately we were not successful at scheduling these
sessions for enough of our participants for this to remain a part of
this protocol.

The following pipeline was set for the audio recordings.

1. vetting and utterance segmentation of session file.

research assistants vet the session recording for any material not
suitable for release. For example, someone saying their address out
loud, conversation considered too intimate, mention of health
conditions, and the like.  In fact such instances were vanishingly
rare.  In this step, researchers also assigned stretches of the
recording to utterances. Our protocols for what exactly counts as an
utterance did evolve somewhat over time and become more precise, so
earlier sessions may not match older sessions.  For example, quoted
material is difficult to assign to utterances ("Brown bear said, get
out of my den! one, two, three!").

2. utterances were then orthographically transcribed in one of 3 ways:
   (a) in-house by lab research assistants
   (b) sent to a commercial company, whose outputs we then corrected
   (c) using Whisper, which we then corrected

3. utterance soundfiles and annotations were then fed through the
Montreal Forced Aligner, which uses a dictionary and a language model
to estimate word and phone boundaries.

4. these MFA outputs were then corrected by hand.

The result is a	time-aligned set of (mostly) maternal utterances, with
an accurate phonological transcription that frequently deviates from
the canonical pronunciation of words. The inspiration for the basic
method was the Buckeye Corpus (Eric Fossler-Lussier, Elizabeth Hume,
Keith Johnson, and Mark	Pitt, with several others:
buckeyecorpus.osu.edu/php/personnel.php )

These steps are very time-consuming, particularly the 4th one. We have
focused on making progress through this pipeline, and as of this
writing have only done superficial quality-checking of the results.

Data are uploaded according to the following organization.

The first level of organization is the dyad; then the age; then the
session.

Within each of these, to the extent that the data are ready, we have

- the audio session file (format .wav)
- a human-corrected TextGrid for each utterance (format ascii textfile, .TextGrid)
- the wav-file for each utterance. (format .wav)
- an excel document giving the notes of the annotator about noise,
  other talkers, sung speech, and the like (format .xlsx)

Note that the textgrids are provided in a single file. To extract them
into individual files, edit and run the script 

scr_unconcatenateTextgrid.py.txt

first by changing its name to remove the ".txt" at the end, and then by
typing "python scr_unconcatenateTextgrid.py" into a command line.

If you are using a computer system that is not python friendly, 
get in touch with the volume maintainer (D. Swingley).
See readme_textgrids_phillyHomeCorpus.txt for more information.
